title: A First Course in Mathematical Modeling
subtitle: International Edition

authors.name: Frank R. Giordano

description: A First Course in Mathematical Modeling is the standard undergraduate introduction to the art and science of building mathematical models. Now in its fifth edition (2013), the book takes a practical, application-driven approach to modeling that emphasizes the modeling process — problem formulation, assumption identification, model construction, solution, validation, and refinement — over mathematical technique. The book is organized around modeling paradigms rather than mathematical subfields: optimization models (linear programming, integer programming, decision analysis), probability models (Monte Carlo simulation, queuing, reliability), and discrete models (difference equations, graph theory, game theory). Each chapter introduces a modeling problem, develops the mathematical tools needed to solve it, works through the solution, and discusses how to validate and refine the model. Real-world applications range from business (inventory management, portfolio optimization) to engineering (circuit design, structural optimization) to social science (voting systems, resource allocation). The authors emphasize sensitivity analysis, model validation, and the iterative nature of modeling throughout. The fifth edition adds new material on game theory, agent-based modeling, and the use of technology (spreadsheets, mathematical software) in the modeling process. The book is designed for a one-semester undergraduate course and assumes only calculus and basic linear algebra.
keyIdeas: The modeling process is iterative: formulate, solve, validate, refine, and repeat until the model is adequate for its purpose
Optimization models find the best solution subject to constraints; linear programming is the most widely used optimization technique
Probability models capture systems with inherent randomness; Monte Carlo simulation uses random sampling to estimate quantities that cannot be computed exactly
Discrete models (difference equations, graph theory, Markov chains) are appropriate when time, state, or space is discrete
Sensitivity analysis — testing how model outputs change when inputs vary — is essential for understanding model robustness
All models are wrong, but some are useful: the goal is not truth but insight that improves decisions
keyTakeaways: Start every modeling problem by writing down your assumptions explicitly — they determine what your model can and cannot capture
Use dimensional analysis to check your equations: every term should have consistent units
For optimization problems, always check whether your solution satisfies the constraints before trusting it
In probability models, the choice of distribution matters enormously — test multiple distributions against your data
Simulation is the method of last resort: use it when analytical solutions are impossible, but be aware that simulation results are noisy
Validation is never finished: every new data point is a potential refutation of your model
whoShouldRead: Undergraduate students in mathematics, engineering, computer science, economics, and operations research
Graduate students entering quantitative fields who need a broad modeling foundation
Practitioners who want a structured approach to building models for real-world problems
Self-learners who want a comprehensive, example-driven introduction to multiple modeling paradigms
whoShouldSkip: Graduate students or researchers seeking advanced theoretical depth in any one subfield
General readers without calculus and basic linear algebra background
Those seeking a specialized text in a single modeling area (optimization only, simulation only)
excerpt: The standard undergraduate textbook on mathematical modeling, covering optimization models, probability models, simulation, and discrete models with real-world applications from business and engineering to social science.
